Alright, you're back and it's time to roll forward. We are at the good stuff now guys where the rubber truly meets the road. Here's the deal. As I said at the end of the last lesson, approximately 20% of the prospects who hit you with an initial objection will end up closing as a result of that one simple loop you just ran. The rest, however, are going to require a bit more persuading in the form you're running in digital loops that are going to address one of the following areas. First, you're going to continue to work on increasing your prospects' level of certainty for the three tenths with every loop. Number two, you're going to work on ratcheting up their pain threshold. In terms of waking up any pain they've buried that's put them in denial. And number three, you're going to be lowering their action threshold. Those are the three things you'll be doing as you run additional loops. Now in terms of number one, increasing that level of certainty for the three tenths, well that's pretty much a given because as I pointed out earlier to you a number of times, certainty wanes. You get to a really, really certain at 3pm and at 3.04 when you're on your next pattern, guess what? That certainty has dropped. Not because you did anything wrong, that's how human beings are built. Until some time has passed and we hear something again and again and again and we form a conviction which is permanent, certainty will wane even after a few minutes. So every time you run a loop, you're always going to make sure that you go back and button up certainty by running one of those additional language patterns that focuses on the three tenths. Make sense? Okay, good. Number two is the pain threshold. While you're going to be waking up their pain, however that being said for strategic reasons, we're not going to focus on that in our second loop. We're going to hold that in reserve. So again, we realize there's pain that's probably been buried, they're in denial and by waking up that pain, yeah, that'll make huge progress in getting them to buy. But for strategic reasons, not yet. We hold it back. And that takes us to number three, which is what we're going to focus on right now in this loop, a second loop, which is lowering our prospects action threshold. You get it? Now here's the deal. I want you to understand that even though that's the focus of this second loop, you need to always remember that you're only spending significant time doing number one as well, meaning you're always going to be building certainty with each loop. Let me explain what I mean. When you're looping, just you understand what's going on here. What you're doing is, is you're essentially looping back into presentation mode in response to getting hit with one of the common objections. In other words, prospect hits you with an objection. Now in the first case with our first loop, what did we do? We deflected the objection and then you ran those three patterns, right? You deflected. Does the idea make sense to you? You limited to your forest gum pattern and built certainty for the second and third ten and then seamlessly transitioned to a close and asked for the order perhaps even engaged in a step down sale if it made sense, right? And that ended loop number one. So then loop number two begins if you get hit with what? One of the common objections. They'll hop to another objection. You get it? Now in that case, you can't deflect the second objection. You have to answer that objection head on. I'll explain that in a moment how we go about doing that, right? But just understand so you can hit with an objection. So you're okay. Well, that begins my next loop. Now in this particular loop, not only will you be building more certainty for the three tens, but you're also going to be specifically focusing on lowering your prospects action threshold. And now since we're talking about definitions, by way of definition, the action threshold is the collective level of certainty that a prospect needs to be at before they feel comfortable enough to buy. Let me repeat that one more time. The action threshold is the collective level of certainty, meaning for all three tens that how they kind of stack up as one unit, right? Collectively, how high a prospect needs to be collectively before they feel comfortable enough to say yes to buy. You get it? Now here's the way this works. I personally, if you recall, have a very low action threshold, which means I don't have to be at a 10, 10, 10 to get me to buy. I could be at a 8, 7, 8, and I'll probably say yes, especially if I'm feeling a bit of pain or it's not that large of a purchase. But you know, 8, 7, 8, 8, 9, 8, 8, 6, I'm probably going to buy. My dad, on the other hand, right, with his ultra high action threshold, he's got to be a 10, 10, 10, and damn sure of it. Bottom line. Now, why is that? Well, let me give you even a better example. Let's just say that my dad and I are facing the same exact decision. I'm at an 8, 8, 8, meaning I'm at an 8 for the product logically and emotionally, just make it easy, right? 8 for the salesperson, largely and emotionally, 8 for the company, 8, 8, 8, 8, across the board, so is my dad. I'll buy, he won't. Why is that? Same level of certainty, one buys, one doesn't. Well, here's why. Remember, in lesson four, module one, I spoke about those parallel movies. Well, watch what happens. So when I'm faced with a buying decision, I will run two movies. I'll run the upside movie. What's the best outcome? If I say yes, what am I going to get if this really works out? How will it improve my, in the case of the golf fryer? How will my golf swing be improved? I had all this stuff going on. I had imagined myself on the 14th, 18th hole of Pebble Beach hitting a perfect four iron. It'll be awesome. Be in the clubhouse toasting with my friends and I had this whole movie I ran. So I run this really long positive movie, right? And of course, I'll also run a downside movie. Now my father, he'll also run an upside movie and a downside movie. In other words, when my father is faced with a buying decision, he runs two movies. He runs the upside movie and he runs the downside movie. Downside being what's the worst that can possibly happen? I too run an upside movie and a downside movie. So what's the difference? Why would I say yes and he say no? Here's why. It's devilishly simple. Because the way I'm built to some of my beliefs about buying, making decisions, just how I feel about the world around my beliefs and values, I end up running this really long positive movie, right? Actually future pace myself and I allow myself to experience all the wonderful things that could happen if it works. And when it gets to the negative movie, I blunt it. I don't run it as long as I should probably. I have a short negative movie, not that intense, and I have a really long wildly intense positive. I'm a glass half full sort of guy. So in that regard, what I do, I buy because I run really long positive movies and really short negative ones. My dad, guess what? He's the opposite. He's got a really short positive movie, meaning he won't allow himself to be insinuated into all the wonderful things that will happen. He'll say, yeah, maybe it'll work. Well, I'll be on the 18th of August through on Pebble Beach. He's like, yeah, maybe it'll help a little bit. And on the downside, I'm like, whatever, I'll lose $49 in the golf swing, right? My dad's like, oh, the guy will steal my credit card. He'll probably want me to put this thing next to him. Worst people will laugh at him. Michael hate me on and before he's done with his negative movie. He's got himself broke, living under a bridge, embarrassed, mortified, and just feeling destitute and alone. That's what high action threshold people do. So that's why we could be at the same level of certainty. And I say, yeah, let's do it. He says, let me think about it. Got to call you back. That's what's going on. Makes sense? This is the crux of how you close anyone who's closed. Because there are so many people like my dad out there where you get to the point in the cell where like, wow, they sound really certain. I know they love the product. I know they kind of trust me too. And they kind of trust the company. They're like, well, fine. Why is that? That's why. Because they're running this really long negative toxic movie and they're not allowing themselves to feel the benefits by running a long positive one. There you go. Now, in reality, how do you lower the action threshold? That's the million dollar question, right? How do you do that? Well, there are actually four ways to do it. So let me go through them right now. I'm going to give you all four. When we get to the four features, the Grand Tamale, it's a big one that really works like unbelievably well. Game jazzy. I'm going to stop, have you take a quiz, and I'll meet you back and really dig into that. All right, so let's start number one. First way is you offer your prospects a money back guarantee. It's very simple, very common strategy. It's used in countless different industries. And it's especially prevalent right now on the internet where the chance of not getting what you thought you were paying for are much higher than in the offline market place where you probably see someone in person, right? It's a local place. So there's lots of large number of overseas vendors online, unlicensed resellers. So there's more skepticism sort of way to get around that is money back guarantee. And it works really well because think about it. How does it work? Well, people say if I get my money back, what's the worst thing that could possibly happen? Not that much. Hence, blunts that negative movie. Somewhat effective, right? Now the second way is to offer your prospects a cooling off or a rescission period. Now this is a contractual feature that allows a prospect to make a binding decision right now on the spot, but then reverse that decision for up to five, seven or 14 days. Now, and again, it depends on the industry. Now this is common in regulated industries like real estate, insurance, vacation sales, mortgages, right? And here's the deal. Wallies are mostly mandated by law. The federal state agencies regulate them, right? It doesn't change the fact that even if it's not mandated, you could still include it in your offer. And it's a very powerful closing tool. Just add it into your terms and conditions and you can use it to close sales. Okay? Very powerful stuff. Now the third way is to use what I call key phrases and language patterns that paint a picture that runs counter to the typical worries and concerns that a high action special prospect tends to ruminate on. Some examples here all hold your hand every step of the way. We pride ourselves on long-term relationships. We work mostly on referral. We have blue chip customer service. There's many of these language patterns. I'll cover all of them a bit later on. We get to another lesson. But I just want you to notice it is effective, but mostly those patterns will be used in conjunction with the fourth way, which is by far the most effective one of all. And what this is is a language pattern that almost borders on a hypnotic pattern. It's not hypnosis, but it's just so powerful that allows you to temporarily reverse a high action threshold prospect strategy for running parallel movies. In other words, you use language to essentially hijack their strategy and you have them run a really long positive movie and you have them blunt the second movie. They run an abbreviated version. And by doing that, whoa, everything changes. It is such a huge difference, but I want to stop right now. I want you to take a short quiz. Not that short actually, right? Go through your exercise. That's some building blocks here. And when you come back, I'm going to dive into this in detail. It's going to be a game changer. I'll see you then.
